title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s signal is unusually concrete: U.S. government evaluators are expanding pre-deployment national security testing with major frontier labs, while also publishing a capability-and-risk assessment of a prominent open-weight model. The rest of the meaningful activity centers on operational risks from autonomous agents, privacy architecture for public sensing, and governance mechanisms for allocating decisions between humans and AI.

items.title: CAISI signs frontier AI national security testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI
items.why_it_matters: It expands formal pre-deployment government testing channels for frontier models with national security relevance.

items.title: CAISI publishes evaluation of DeepSeek V4 Pro
items.why_it_matters: It adds a public government benchmark for frontier-adjacent open-weight model capabilities and associated risk posture.

items.title: Autonomous LLM agent worms study cross-platform propagation and temporal re-entry defenses
items.why_it_matters: It highlights a new persistence and propagation threat model for long-running AI agents.

items.title: CityOS proposes a privacy architecture for urban sensing
items.why_it_matters: It addresses policy enforcement, retention, and access controls for increasingly pervasive public-space sensing.

items.title: HAAS introduces a policy-aware framework for adaptive task allocation between humans and AI
items.why_it_matters: It targets governance of when humans versus AI should decide under varying stakes and constraints.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
